The wi_skills run produced a slightly more detailed and longer document (4128 bytes vs 3120 bytes) with four clearly separated draft reply sections and more explicit pending-approval labeling throughout. The wo_skills run was more concise but equally functional, and notably caught the buffer timing issue more precisely (flagging 60-min gap vs required 75-min). Overall, wi_skills is marginally more thorough in presentation, but the practical difference is minimal since the key skill-enabled behaviors (real conflict detection, actual calendar consultation) were not achieved in either run due to unavailable GWS integrations.

utility_details.utility_summary: Overall, both wi_skills and wo_skills produced structurally similar outputs because neither agent was able to invoke the GWS workflows that would have grounded the content in real data. The wi_skills agent did correctly invoke the persona-exec-assistant skill and followed its prerequisite chain, but the underlying GWS utility skills were unavailable, forcing a fallback. The wo_skills agent skipped skill invocation entirely and went straight to template generation. In practice, the quality difference is minimal: both produced generic weekly digests with placeholder data, generic board pre-reads without real attendees or linked documents, and similar decisive email drafts. The wo_skills output is actually slightly more detailed and better formatted (with emoji urgency markers, more explicit placeholder annotations, and more elaborate board prep content at 7141 bytes vs 5025 bytes), but both fail the data-grounding criteria for J1, J2, and J5. Neither output demonstrates the skill's intended advantage of chaining real GWS workflow data into the document. Conclusion: wi_skills and wo_skills are roughly equivalent in quality for this run, with neither successfully leveraging live workflow data. The skill provided no measurable benefit due to missing GWS tool dependencies.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective overall. The key differentiator is J1 (conflict detection) and J5 (weekly agenda coherence): wi_skills invoked the gws calendar CLI to retrieve real calendar data, correctly identified the Tuesday 10:00 AM conflict with the All-Hands Prep Session, proposed an alternative time, and built a week-at-a-glance grounded in actual events. wo_skills lacked real calendar data access, missed the Tuesday conflict entirely (recommending acceptance of the conflicting slot), and its weekly view was self-admittedly fabricated without live data. Both agents handled executive confirmation discipline, reply draft quality, preference adherence, and multi-request completeness adequately. The skill-enabled agent scored 6/6 while the baseline scored 4/6, with critical failures on conflict detection and calendar coherence.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is clearly more effective. It successfully invoked all three GWS workflows (weekly-digest, meeting-prep, gmail triage) to retrieve real mock data, producing a digest with 3 specific decisions, a board pre-read with a full 10-person attendee list and 3 named documents, and calendar-grounded time slots for the scheduling email. It also clearly differentiated urgency levels for weekend action items. The wo_skills agent, lacking the skill and GWS CLI access, was limited to content from flagged_emails.md alone — it produced good email drafts (J3 passes) and adequate formatting (J6 passes), but failed on digest completeness (J1), board pre-read specificity (J2), verified calendar availability for scheduling (J4), and urgency differentiation grounded in workflow data (J5). The skill made a decisive difference in 4 of 6 evaluation dimensions.
